activities_projects.title: Electrochemical Impulse Modeling
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the transmission of nerve impulses across a synapse
steps: Model the structure of a synapse, including the axon terminal, synaptic cleft, and dendrite.
Identify the 'electrical-to-chemical' conversion process at the terminal.
Research how calcium ions ($Ca^{2+}$) trigger the release of neurotransmitters.
Explain the 'one-way' nature of nerve impulse transmission based on the absence of chemical receptors at the axon end.
Calculate the approximate speed of an impulse in a myelinated vs. non-myelinated neuron.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the molecular mechanism of synaptic transmission and the directionality of the nervous system.
activities_projects.evaluation: Technical explanation of neurotransmitter release and receptor binding (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Quantitative Auxin Gradient Analysis
activities_projects.objective: Mathematically model how auxin concentration affects cell elongation in roots vs. shoots
steps: Plot a graph showing the percentage of cell elongation vs. auxin concentration for both shoots and roots.
Identify the 'Optimal Concentration' for each (shoots require higher, roots require lower).
Explain why the same hormone (Auxin) inhibits root growth while promoting shoot growth on the shaded side.
Relate this to the mechanism of positive geotropism in roots.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the differential sensitivity of plant tissues to hormonal concentrations.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct interpretation of the 'Inhibition vs. Stimulation' curve for roots and shoots (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Homeostasis and Negative Feedback Loops
activities_projects.objective: Map the complex interaction between the Hypothalamus, Pituitary, and Target Glands
steps: Draw a multi-level feedback loop involving TRH (Hypothalamus), TSH (Pituitary), and Thyroxine (Thyroid).
Identify the 'inhibitory' signal sent when Thyroxine levels exceed the threshold.
Discuss the consequences of a 'malfunctioning feedback' resulting in Hyperthyroidism.
Explain how this system maintains a constant metabolic rate.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes the concept of hierarchical control and negative feedback in the endocrine system.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the multi-gland feedback diagram and identification of inhibitory pathways (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Mechanism of 'Thigmonasty' in Mimosa
activities_projects.objective: Investigate the role of Pulvini and electrical signals in rapid plant movement
steps: Trigger a leaf fold and measure the speed of the signal as it travels to adjacent leaves.
Apply an ice cube to the stem and observe if it triggers the same response as a touch.
Research how the loss of turgor pressure in the 'pulvinus' (leaf base) causes drooping.
Explain how ions ($K^+$ and $Cl^-$) move out of cells to facilitate water loss.
Compare this rapid electrical-chemical signaling in plants to animal nervous systems.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that plants utilize electrical-chemical means to convey information without a specialized nervous system.
activities_projects.evaluation: Technical report on turgor pressure changes in the pulvinus during nastic movement (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Brain Plasticity and Learning
activities_projects.objective: Investigate how the Cerebrum adapts to new motor skills
steps: Attempt to draw a star while looking only at its reflection in a mirror.
Record the time and the number of errors (lines outside the boundary) for 10 consecutive trials.
Graph the 'Learning Curve'.
Identify which part of the brain (Cerebellum vs. Cerebrum) is primarily involved in 'fine-tuning' the motor response through repetition.
Discuss the concept of 'neural pathways' becoming stronger with use.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Relates the concept of coordination to the adaptive capacity of the hindbrain and forebrain.
activities_projects.evaluation: Analysis of the learning curve and the role of the Cerebellum in motor coordination (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Hormonal Antagonism: Glucagon vs. Insulin
activities_projects.objective: Model the dual-hormone control of blood glucose
materials: Data on blood sugar levels after fasting vs. after a high-carb meal
steps: Plot a double-line graph showing blood glucose and insulin levels over 4 hours after a meal.
Indicate the point where Glucagon secretion would theoretically begin as glucose drops.
Explain why this is an 'antagonistic' relationship.
Research Type 1 vs. Type 2 Diabetes in the context of receptor sensitivity vs. hormone production.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between the roles of Alpha and Beta cells of the Islets of Langerhans.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of antagonistic hormonal peaks and troughs on the graph (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Cerebrospinal Fluid (CSF) and Protection
activities_projects.objective: Research the mechanical and chemical shielding of the Central Nervous System
steps: Simulate the brain in the skull: Place an egg in a jar. Shake it once dry (it breaks). Fill the jar with water and shake again (it survives).
Identify the three layers of the Meninges.
Research the 'Blood-Brain Barrier' and why certain chemicals cannot reach the brain.
Explain the role of CSF in buoyancy and nutrient transport.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the structural and physiological defenses that protect the delicate nervous tissue.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of the physical and chemical protective mechanisms of the CNS (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Photoperiodism: The Clock in Plants
activities_projects.objective: Analyze how plants 'measure' day length for flowering
materials: Research data on Long-day and Short-day plants
steps: Identify the role of 'Phytochrome' pigments in detecting light.
Explain how the length of the 'uninterrupted dark period' is the critical factor, not the day length.
Analyze a case study where a flash of light in the middle of the night prevents a short-day plant from flowering.
Relate this to 'chemical coordination' over a long-term time scale.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that plants possess complex molecular 'clocks' to coordinate seasonal responses.
activities_projects.evaluation: Detailed reasoning on the importance of the dark period for flowering (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Endocrine Disruptors: A Case Study
activities_projects.objective: Investigate how external chemicals mimic or block hormones
materials: Case study on Bisphenol A (BPA) or Pesticides
steps: Research how BPA mimics Estrogen and its effect on the reproductive system.
Identify which glands are most susceptible to environmental toxins.
Explain the concept of 'Hormone Mimicry'.
Draft a scientific argument for banning certain plastics based on endocrine health.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies understanding of hormone-receptor binding to real-world environmental health issues.
activities_projects.evaluation: Scientific summary of the mechanism of action of endocrine disruptors (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Comparative Analysis of Coordination Systems
activities_projects.objective: Contrast the speed, reach, and duration of Nervous vs. Hormonal signals
steps: Compare the 'Nervous System' and 'Endocrine System' across four criteria: Speed of signal, Duration of effect, Pathway (wires vs. blood), and Target (localized vs. widespread).
Explain why 'emergency' responses use both systems simultaneously (Adrenaline).
Discuss why growth is controlled by hormones and not nerves.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes a holistic view of how multicellular organisms integrate multiple control systems.
activities_projects.evaluation: Completed comparison table with at least four distinct parameters (10 marks)
